# Prompt 05 — Implement PR

## Purpose

Use this prompt to implement one approved pull request from an existing PR breakdown.

The goal is to make the smallest safe code or documentation change needed for the selected PR, while preserving existing behavior and keeping the result easy to review, validate, and revert.

This prompt must not be used to implement multiple PRs at once.

## When to use

Use this prompt when:

- a requirement already exists;
- a functional specification already exists;
- a technical plan already exists;
- a PR breakdown already exists;
- one specific PR has been selected for implementation;
- the expected scope, files, validation, and regression risks are known.

Do not use this prompt to redesign the architecture, expand scope, or implement future PRs.

## Recommended skill

Use this prompt with the specialist skill that matches the selected PR.

Common options:

```txt
.agents/skills/frontend-implementer/SKILL.md
.agents/skills/backend-implementer/SKILL.md
.agents/skills/docs-writer/SKILL.md
.agents/skills/build-and-validate/SKILL.md
.agents/skills/deploy-engineer/SKILL.md
```

Supporting skills:

```txt
.agents/skills/pr-orchestrator/SKILL.md
.agents/skills/tester/SKILL.md
```

Use `pr-orchestrator` to keep the PR scoped.

Use `tester` to check whether the implementation satisfies the PR acceptance criteria.

## Required input

Before running this prompt, provide:

```md
# Implement PR Input

## Selected PR

[Paste the selected PR from the PR breakdown]

## Requirement

[Paste or reference the approved requirement]

## Functional specification

[Paste or reference the approved functional specification]

## Technical plan

[Paste or reference the approved technical plan]

## PR breakdown

[Paste or reference the approved PR breakdown]

## Current project context

[Relevant files, structure, commands, existing patterns, or repository notes]

## Constraints

[No-regression rules, compatibility requirements, coding standards, security constraints, design constraints, or delivery limits]

## Validation expectations

[Known commands, manual checks, CI checks, or required validation]

## Out of scope

[What must not be changed in this implementation]

## Notes

[Any assumptions, known risks, related files, screenshots, logs, or previous decisions]
```

## Instructions for Codex

You are implementing one selected pull request.

Follow these rules:

1. Implement only the selected PR.
2. Preserve the approved scope.
3. Do not implement future PRs.
4. Do not introduce unrelated refactors.
5. Do not add dependencies unless the selected PR explicitly requires it.
6. Do not change architecture unless the technical plan explicitly requires it.
7. Follow existing project patterns.
8. Preserve existing behavior unless the selected PR explicitly changes it.
9. Keep the change small and reviewable.
10. Update tests or documentation only when required by the selected PR.
11. Validate the change using the defined validation plan when possible.
12. List all changed files.
13. List untested areas.
14. List regression risks.
15. Do not claim tests passed without evidence.
16. Do not make commits unless explicitly instructed.

## Implementation process

Follow this process:

1. Restate the selected PR objective.
2. Confirm included and excluded scope.
3. Inspect relevant files and existing patterns.
4. Identify the smallest safe change.
5. Implement the change.
6. Review changed files for unrelated modifications.
7. Run relevant validation when possible.
8. Document executed and non-executed checks.
9. Document regression risks.
10. Prepare an implementation summary.

## Output format

Codex should respond using this structure:

```md
# PR Implementation Result

## Summary

Short explanation of what was implemented.

## Selected PR

- PR title:
- Objective:

## Scope

### Included

- Item 1
- Item 2

### Not included

- Item 1
- Item 2

## Files changed

- `path/to/file` — what changed and why

## Behavior preserved

- Existing behavior that should remain unchanged

## Behavior changed

- Changed behavior, if any

## Validation

### Executed

| Check   | Command / Method | Result          |
| ------- | ---------------- | --------------- |
| Check 1 | `command`        | Passed / Failed |

### Not executed

- Check not executed — reason

## Findings

### Finding 1

Severity: High | Medium | Low
Type: Confirmed issue | Hypothesis | Observation

Description:
Explain the issue clearly.

Evidence:
Show the evidence.

Impact:
Explain why it matters.

Recommended action:
Explain the smallest safe next step.

## Regression risks

- Risk 1
- Risk 2

## Assumptions

- Assumption 1
- Assumption 2

## Final status

Ready for review | Needs changes | Blocked
```

## Scope control rules

The implementation must stay inside the selected PR.

If Codex discovers work that belongs to another PR, it must not implement it automatically.

Instead, document it under:

```md
## Out of scope discoveries

- Discovery 1 — recommended future PR or follow-up
```

Examples of out-of-scope discoveries:

- unrelated bug;
- missing refactor opportunity;
- dependency upgrade opportunity;
- broader architecture issue;
- missing feature not required by the selected PR;
- additional design improvement;
- deployment improvement unrelated to this PR.

## Existing pattern rule

Before changing files, Codex should identify the relevant existing pattern.

Examples:

- how components are organized;
- how services are created;
- how repositories are named;
- how routes are protected;
- how API calls are made;
- how tests are written;
- how errors are handled;
- how commands are defined;
- how documentation is structured.

Prefer existing patterns over new conventions.

## No-regression rule

Existing behavior must be preserved unless explicitly changed by the selected PR.

When behavior changes, document:

- old behavior;
- new behavior;
- reason for change;
- validation performed.

Do not silently change behavior.

## Dependency rule

Do not add, remove, or upgrade dependencies unless the selected PR explicitly requires it.

If a dependency change appears necessary, stop and report:

```md
## Dependency decision required

- Dependency:
- Why it appears necessary:
- Risk:
- Alternative without dependency:
- Recommendation:
```

## Test and validation rule

Run or recommend the smallest relevant validation set.

Prefer commands defined by the project.

Common examples:

```bash
npm run lint
npm run typecheck
npm test
npm run build
```

Use the project’s package manager.

Do not invent command results.

If commands cannot be run, state why and provide manual validation steps.

## Failure handling

If validation fails, do not hide it.

Classify the failure:

- build failure;
- type failure;
- lint failure;
- test failure;
- runtime failure;
- environment failure;
- documentation failure.

Then provide:

- evidence;
- likely cause;
- smallest recommended fix;
- how to validate after the fix.

## Documentation changes

Update documentation only when:

- the selected PR changes workflow;
- the selected PR changes setup;
- the selected PR changes commands;
- the selected PR changes public behavior;
- the selected PR explicitly includes documentation.

Avoid documentation rewrites unrelated to the PR.

## Testing changes

Update or add tests when:

- the selected PR changes behavior;
- the selected PR fixes a bug;
- the selected PR adds a new user or system flow;
- the selected PR changes validation logic;
- the selected PR affects a critical path.

Do not add tests that only lock irrelevant implementation details.

## Quality criteria

The implementation is acceptable when:

- it implements only the selected PR;
- it follows the approved requirement, spec, technical plan, and PR breakdown;
- scope is controlled;
- changed files are relevant;
- existing patterns are followed;
- behavior changes are explicit;
- no unrelated changes are introduced;
- validation is executed or clearly marked as not executed;
- regression risks are listed;
- no high-severity issue remains.

## Common mistakes to avoid

- implementing multiple PRs at once;
- expanding scope because the change “seems easy”;
- adding dependencies without approval;
- mixing refactor with feature implementation;
- rewriting unrelated files;
- silently changing behavior;
- ignoring existing project patterns;
- skipping validation;
- claiming tests passed without evidence;
- hiding untested areas;
- making commits without explicit instruction.

## Example usage

```md
Use `prompts/05-implement-pr.md`.

# Implement PR Input

## Selected PR

PR 1 — Protect dashboard routes

Objective:
Ensure unauthenticated users are redirected to `/login` when accessing `/dashboard`.

Scope:
Included:

- Add or update route middleware for dashboard protection.
- Reuse existing session composable.

Not included:

- Role-based permissions.
- User profile.
- Password reset.

Expected files:

- `app/middleware/auth.ts`
- `app/pages/dashboard/index.vue`

Validation:

- `npm run lint`
- `npm run typecheck`
- `npm run build`
- Manual check: unauthenticated user opening `/dashboard` redirects to `/login`.

## Requirement

Authenticated users should be able to access the dashboard. Unauthenticated users should be redirected to login.

## Functional specification

Given an unauthenticated user, when the user opens `/dashboard`, then the system redirects to `/login`.

Given an authenticated user, when the user opens `/dashboard`, then the dashboard renders.

## Technical plan

Use existing session composable and route middleware. Do not add role-based permissions.

## PR breakdown

PR 1 implements only dashboard route protection.

## Current project context

Nuxt application with existing session composable and dashboard page.

## Constraints

Do not redesign authentication. Do not add roles.

## Validation expectations

Run lint, typecheck, build, and manual route checks.

## Out of scope

Role-based permissions, user profile, password reset.

## Notes

Keep this PR small.
```

## Final instruction

Implement only the selected PR.

Do not continue to review, fix, deploy, or implement the next PR unless explicitly requested.
